OS Prep 🎯
CHAPTER 04 · CO4
Deadlock Management
Principles · Conditions · RAG · Prevention · Avoidance · Detection · Recovery · Dining Philosophers
Core Concepts
What is a Deadlock?
A deadlock is a situation where a set of processes are permanently blocked, each waiting for a resource held by another process in the same set. No process can ever proceed — they're stuck forever.

A process uses a resource in 3 steps: Request → Use → Release.
Example: P1 holds Printer, wants DVD. P2 holds DVD, wants Printer → Deadlock!
4 Necessary Conditions for Deadlock (Coffman's Conditions)
ALL four must hold simultaneously for a deadlock to occur:

1. Mutual Exclusion — At least one resource is non-shareable (only one process at a time).
2. Hold and Wait — A process holds at least one resource AND waits to acquire more held by others.
3. No Preemption — A resource can only be released voluntarily by the process holding it.
4. Circular Wait — A chain of processes P0→P1→P2→...→P0, each waiting for a resource held by the next.
Memory trick + exam note
Remember as "MH-NC" → Mutual exclusion, Hold-and-wait, No preemption, Circular wait.

Exam tip: "Circular wait implies hold-and-wait" — they are not fully independent. Breaking ANY ONE condition prevents deadlock.
Resource Allocation Graph (RAG)
A directed graph with two types of nodes and two types of edges:

Nodes: Processes (circles) and Resources (rectangles with dots = instances)
Request edge: Pi → Rj — Process Pi is requesting resource Rj
Assignment edge: Rj → Pi — Resource Rj is allocated to Pi

Rules:
• If graph has NO cycle → No deadlock guaranteed
• If graph has a cycle + single instance per resource → Deadlock
• If graph has a cycle + multiple instances → Deadlock may or may not exist
Wait-for Graph (for Detection)
A Wait-for Graph is derived from the RAG by removing resource nodes and collapsing edges. An edge Pi → Pj means Pi is waiting for Pj to release a resource.

If the wait-for graph contains a cycle → Deadlock (used only when each resource has single instance).
Detection runs in O(n²) operations.
Deadlock Prevention
Prevention — Attack each condition
Break one of the 4 conditions:
ConditionPrevention StrategyDrawback
Mutual ExclusionMake resources shareable (e.g., read-only files)Not possible for all resources (printers can't be shared)
Hold & WaitRequest ALL resources before start, OR release all before requesting new onesLow utilization; starvation possible
No PreemptionIf a resource can't be granted, preempt (take away) resources of waiting processesOnly works for resources whose state can be saved (CPU regs, memory)
Circular WaitImpose a total ordering on resources. Processes must request in increasing order (F: tape=1, disk=5, printer=12)Hard to enforce; app developer responsibility
Deadlock Avoidance — Banker's Algorithm
Safe State Concept
A safe state = the OS can find a safe sequence <P1, P2, ..., Pn> where each process can be satisfied using currently available resources + resources freed by earlier processes.

Safe → No deadlock guaranteed
Unsafe → Deadlock POSSIBLE (but not certain)

Banker's Algorithm keeps the system always in a safe state by checking before granting any resource.
Banker's Algorithm — Data Structures
For n processes and m resource types:

Available[m] — Number of free instances of each resource type
Max[n][m] — Maximum demand of each process for each resource
Allocation[n][m] — Currently allocated resources to each process
Need[n][m] — Remaining need: Need = Max − Allocation
Safety Algorithm
1. Work = Available; Finish[i] = false for all i
2. Find i where Finish[i]=false AND Need[i] ≤ Work
3. Work = Work + Allocation[i]; Finish[i] = true; goto step 2
4. If Finish[i] = true for ALL i → SAFE STATE ✓
// Complexity: O(m × n²)
Resource-Request Algorithm (when Pi requests Requesti)
1. If Request[i] > Need[i] → ERROR (exceeded max claim)
2. If Request[i] > Available → Pi must WAIT
3. Pretend to allocate:
  Available = Available − Request[i]
  Allocation[i] = Allocation[i] + Request[i]
  Need[i] = Need[i] − Request[i]
4. Run Safety Algorithm:
  If SAFE → Grant request ✓
  If UNSAFE → Rollback and make Pi wait
📊 Classic Exam Example — Banker's (5 processes, 3 resources A,B,C)
Alloc Max Available A B C A B C A B C P0 0 1 0 7 5 3 3 3 2 P1 2 0 0 3 2 2 P2 3 0 2 9 0 2 P3 2 1 1 2 2 2 P4 0 0 2 4 3 3 Need (= Max − Alloc): P0 = 7 4 3 P1 = 1 2 2 ← Need ≤ Available? Yes! → allocate P1 first P2 = 6 0 0 P3 = 0 1 1 ← Next P4 = 4 3 1 Safe Sequence: <P1, P3, P4, P2, P0> ✓

If P1 now requests (1,0,2): Check (1,0,2) ≤ Need(1,2,2) ✓ and ≤ Available(3,3,2) ✓ → pretend allocate → run safety → Safe sequence found → GRANT ✓

Deadlock Detection & Recovery
Detection Algorithm (Multiple Instances)
Similar to Banker's Safety Algorithm but uses Request matrix (current requests) instead of Need. If Finish[i] = false after running → those processes are deadlocked.

Key difference from Banker's: No Max declared. The algorithm is optimistic — assumes processes with no current requests will finish soon.
When to invoke detection?
• Every resource request that can't be immediately granted — catches deadlock fast but expensive
• Periodically (e.g., every hour) or when CPU utilization drops below 40%

Trade-off: More frequent = faster detection but higher overhead.
Recovery from Deadlock
Two approaches:

1. Process Termination
  • Abort ALL deadlocked processes (expensive — discards work)
  • Abort ONE at a time until deadlock breaks (run detection after each abort)
  • Victim selection based on: priority, computation time, resources held, resources needed

2. Resource Preemption
  • Select a victim → Rollback → Restart from a safe state
  • Risk of starvation (same process always picked as victim) — solved by including rollback count in cost factor
Dining Philosophers Problem
Dining Philosophers — Classic Synchronization Problem
Setup: 5 philosophers sitting at a round table. 5 chopsticks (one between each pair). Each philosopher needs 2 chopsticks to eat. They think → pick up left → pick up right → eat → put down.

Problem: If all 5 pick up left chopstick simultaneously → all wait for right → DEADLOCK

Solutions:
• Allow at most 4 philosophers at the table at once
• Pick up both chopsticks atomically (only if both available)
• Asymmetric: Odd-numbered philosophers pick left first; even pick right first (breaks circular wait)
• Monitor-based: Philosopher picks up both or waits
Mapping to 4 deadlock conditions
Mutual exclusion: Chopstick can only be used by one philosopher
Hold & wait: Philosopher holds one chopstick and waits for another
No preemption: Philosopher won't put down chopstick until done eating
Circular wait: P1 waits for P2's chopstick, P2 waits for P3's... P5 waits for P1's
Quick Comparison Table
ApproachMechanismOverheadDrawback
PreventionDeny one of 4 conditionsLow at runtimePoor utilization / starvation
Avoidance (Banker)Safe state checkingMediumNeed max declared in advance
DetectionRun detection algorithmHighRecovery is expensive
IgnoreOstrich algorithmNoneSystem may crash (used in UNIX/Windows!)
Important Q&A
Q1What are the 4 necessary conditions for deadlock? (2 marks)
The four necessary conditions are: (1) Mutual Exclusion — at least one resource must be non-shareable; (2) Hold and Wait — a process holds one resource while waiting for others; (3) No Preemption — resources can only be released voluntarily; (4) Circular Wait — a circular chain of processes, each waiting for a resource held by the next. All four must hold simultaneously for deadlock to occur.
Q2Explain the Banker's Algorithm with an example. (10 marks)
The Banker's Algorithm is a deadlock avoidance algorithm. It is named because it works like a bank that never allocates cash in a way that it can't satisfy all future needs.

Data structures: Available, Max, Allocation, Need (= Max − Allocation).

Working: When a process requests resources, the OS pretends to allocate them and runs the Safety Algorithm. If a safe sequence exists, it grants the request. Otherwise, the process must wait.

See the worked example above for the 5-process, 3-resource illustration.

Limitations: Every process must declare maximum resource needs in advance. Fixed number of processes and resources assumed.
Q3Differentiate deadlock prevention vs deadlock avoidance. (4 marks)
Prevention — Structurally ensures one of the 4 conditions can never hold (e.g., resource ordering). Applied at design time. Simpler but causes low resource utilization.

Avoidance — Dynamically decides at runtime whether to grant a request based on future knowledge (max claims). Uses Banker's Algorithm. More flexible but requires advance information and has runtime overhead.
Q4What is a Resource Allocation Graph? When does it indicate deadlock? (5 marks)
A Resource Allocation Graph (RAG) is a directed graph with two types of nodes — Processes (circles) and Resources (rectangles). Two types of edges: Request edge (Pi → Rj) and Assignment edge (Rj → Pi).

Deadlock detection from RAG:
• No cycle → No deadlock
• Cycle + single instance per resource → Deadlock definitely exists
• Cycle + multiple instances → Deadlock may or may not exist (need to check further)
Q5Consider: Available = [0,0,0]; 3 processes. P0: Alloc=[1,0,0] Max=[1,0,0]; P1: Alloc=[0,1,0] Max=[0,1,0]; P2: Alloc=[0,0,1] Max=[0,0,1]. Is system in safe state? (5 marks)
Need = Max − Allocation: P0 needs [0,0,0], P1 needs [0,0,0], P2 needs [0,0,0].

Since all Need values = [0,0,0] and Work starts at [0,0,0]:
• P0: Need [0,0,0] ≤ Work [0,0,0] ✓ → Work becomes [0,0,0]+[1,0,0] = [1,0,0]
• P1: Need [0,0,0] ≤ Work [1,0,0] ✓ → Work = [1,1,0]
• P2: Need [0,0,0] ≤ Work [1,1,0] ✓ → Work = [1,1,1]

All Finish[i] = true. Safe state — Safe sequence <P0, P1, P2>.
Q6Explain the Dining Philosophers problem and its deadlock-free solutions. (7 marks)
5 philosophers at a round table, alternating between thinking and eating. 5 chopsticks, one between each pair. A philosopher needs both adjacent chopsticks to eat.

Deadlock scenario: All pick up left chopstick simultaneously → circular wait → deadlock.

Solutions:
1. Allow max 4 philosophers at table at once (breaks circular wait by not allowing full circle to form)
2. Atomic pickup — Only pick up chopsticks if both are free (breaks hold-and-wait)
3. Asymmetric solution — Odd philosophers pick left then right; even pick right then left (breaks circular wait)
4. Monitor-based — Use a monitor with states (thinking/hungry/eating) and condition variables

Note: Simply always having philosophers pick up left before right causes deadlock. The asymmetric approach is the simplest fix.
CHAPTER 05 · CO5
Memory Management
Partitioning · Paging · Segmentation · TLB · Virtual Memory · Page Replacement
Core Concepts
Why Memory Management?
The CPU can ONLY access data in main memory and registers. For multiprogramming (multiple processes in RAM), the OS must:

• Allocate & deallocate memory efficiently
• Protect one process's memory from another
• Enable processes larger than physical memory (virtual memory)

Key concepts: Logical address (CPU generates) vs Physical address (actual RAM). The MMU (Memory Management Unit) translates between them.
Address Binding — When are addresses translated?
1. Compile time — Absolute addresses generated. If start location changes, must recompile. (Old MS-DOS)
2. Load time — Relocatable code; final binding at load. If start changes, reload only.
3. Execution time — Binding delayed to runtime (most modern OSes). Requires hardware (MMU). Allows process to move in memory.
Memory Partitioning
Fixed Partitioning
Memory divided into fixed-size partitions. Each partition holds exactly one process.

Problem: Internal Fragmentation — Allocated memory > needed memory. Wasted space inside a partition.
Simple to implement but inflexible.
Dynamic Partitioning
Partitions created on demand with exact size. Memory maintained as list of free holes.

Problem: External Fragmentation — Total free memory is enough but it's scattered in small pieces (not contiguous).
Solution: Compaction — Shuffle processes to merge free holes into one big block. Requires dynamic relocation. Expensive!
Memory Allocation Strategies
StrategyDescriptionAdvantageDisadvantage
First FitAllocate the first hole big enoughFastest searchLeaves small leftover holes at start
Best FitAllocate the smallest hole that's big enoughMinimal leftover per allocationSlow (search entire list); creates tiny unusable holes
Worst FitAllocate the largest holeLarge leftover hole (potentially reusable)Slowest; wastes big holes
💡
Simulation studies: First Fit and Best Fit both beat Worst Fit in utilization. First Fit is generally faster than Best Fit. The 50% rule: ~1/3 of memory may be unusable due to fragmentation with First Fit.
Paging
Paging — Basic Concept
Physical memory divided into fixed-size blocks = Frames
Logical memory divided into same-size blocks = Pages

Pages of a process can be loaded into any available frames (non-contiguous). The Page Table maps page numbers to frame numbers.

A logical address is split into: Page number (p) + Page offset (d)
Physical address = Frame_base_address + offset
📐 Address Translation Example
Page size = 4 bytes, Physical memory = 32 bytes (8 frames) Logical address 13: 13 in binary = 1101 With 4-byte pages → page bits = 2 (since 4=2²) Page number (p) = 13 / 4 = 3 Page offset (d) = 13 % 4 = 1 Page table: Page 3 → Frame 2 Physical address = 2×4 + 1 = 9 ✓
Paging — Fragmentation
✅ No external fragmentation — Any free frame can be used for any page
⚠️ Internal fragmentation — Last page of a process may not be full
Example: Process needs 72,766 bytes, page size = 2048 bytes → needs 35.5 pages → 36 pages allocated → internal fragmentation = 2048 − (72766 − 35×2048) = 962 bytes
TLB — Translation Look-aside Buffer
Problem with page tables: Stored in main memory → every address translation requires 2 memory accesses (page table + actual data) → slow!

TLB = A small, fast associative cache that stores recent page-to-frame mappings.

TLB Hit → Frame found directly, only 1 memory access needed
TLB Miss → Access page table in memory, then update TLB
Effective Access Time (EAT) Calculation
Let α = hit ratio, t = TLB search time, m = memory access time

EAT = α(t + m) + (1-α)(t + 2m)

Example: α = 0.80, t = 20ns, m = 100ns
EAT = 0.80(20+100) + 0.20(20+100+100)
      = 0.80×120 + 0.20×220
      = 96 + 44 = 140 ns (40% slower than 100ns ideal)
Segmentation
Segmentation
Logical memory divided into variable-size segments (code, data, stack, heap). Matches programmer's view of memory.

A logical address = <segment number, offset>
Segment Table has base (starting physical address) and limit (length) for each segment.

Physical address = segment base + offset (if offset < limit, else trap)

✅ No internal fragmentation
⚠️ External fragmentation possible (variable sizes)
✅ Matches logical structure (function = segment)
FeaturePagingSegmentation
DivisionFixed-size pagesVariable-size segments
FragmentationInternal onlyExternal only
Programmer visible?No (transparent)Yes (logical units)
Address formatPage # + offsetSegment # + offset
ProtectionPer page bitsPer segment (read/write/execute)
Virtual Memory & Demand Paging
Virtual Memory — Core Idea
Virtual memory allows programs larger than physical RAM to run. Only the needed parts (pages) are loaded into memory. Rest stays on disk.

Demand Paging — Load a page only when it is accessed (demanded). A valid-invalid bit in the page table indicates if a page is in memory (valid) or on disk (invalid).

Page Fault — When a page is accessed but not in memory → OS loads it from disk → expensive (I/O operation!)
Page Fault Handling Steps
1. Process accesses a page → MMU checks valid-invalid bit
2. Bit = invalid → PAGE FAULT trap to OS
3. OS finds page on disk (backing store)
4. Find a free frame (or select victim frame via page replacement)
5. Load page from disk into frame
6. Update page table; set valid bit
7. Restart the instruction that caused fault
Page Replacement Algorithms
FIFO (First-In First-Out)
Replace the page that has been in memory the longest. Simple queue.

⚠️ Belady's Anomaly — More frames can sometimes cause MORE page faults! (Unique to FIFO)
Optimal Page Replacement (OPT)
Replace the page that will not be used for the longest time in the future.

✅ Minimum page faults guaranteed
❌ Not implementable in practice (requires future knowledge)
Used as a benchmark to evaluate other algorithms.
LRU (Least Recently Used)
Replace the page that has not been used for the longest time in the past. Based on principle of locality.

✅ Good performance, close to Optimal
❌ Implementation expensive (needs timestamp or stack)
No Belady's anomaly (stack algorithm)
📊 Page Replacement Example — Reference string: 7,0,1,2,0,3,0,4,2,3,0,3,2 | Frames = 3
FIFO: 7 0 1 | 2 0 3 | 0 4 2 | 3 0 3 | 2 7 7 7 2 2 2 0 0 0 3 3 3 3 0 0 0 0 0 3 3 3 3 0 0 0 1 1 1 3 3 4 4 4 4 4 2 F F F F H F F F H F F H F Total page faults with FIFO = 9 LRU: 7→F, 0→F, 1→F, 2→F(rep 7), 0→H, 3→F(rep 1), 0→H, 4→F(rep 2), 2→F(rep 3), 3→F(rep 0), 0→F(rep 2), 3→H, 2→F Total page faults with LRU = 8
AlgorithmReplaceBelady?Implementable?Performance
FIFOOldest loaded pageYes ⚠️Yes (easy)Worst
OPTFurthest future useNoNo (theoretical)Best
LRULeast recently usedNoYes (complex)Near-optimal
Thrashing
Thrashing occurs when a process spends more time paging (swapping) than executing — CPU utilization drops dramatically.

Cause: Too many processes in memory → each has too few frames → constant page faults
Detection: CPU utilization drops even as degree of multiprogramming increases
Solution: Working Set Model — Keep the "working set" (recently used pages) of each process in memory. If total working sets > available frames → suspend some processes.
Important Q&A
Q1Explain paging and how logical address is translated to physical address. (8 marks)
Paging is a memory management scheme that allows the physical address space of a process to be non-contiguous. Physical memory is divided into fixed-size blocks called frames; logical memory into same-size blocks called pages.

Address Translation:
The logical address (generated by CPU) is divided into: Page number (p) = high-order bits, Page offset (d) = low-order bits.
If logical address space = 2m and page size = 2n: high (m-n) bits = page number, low n bits = offset.

The page table is indexed with p to get the frame number f.
Physical address = f × page_size + d

Advantages: No external fragmentation, simple memory allocation
Disadvantages: Internal fragmentation in last page; page table overhead
Q2What is TLB? How does it improve memory access? Calculate EAT. (6 marks)
A TLB (Translation Look-aside Buffer) is a small, fast associative cache that stores recently used page-to-frame mappings. It solves the problem of 2 memory accesses needed with page tables stored in RAM.

Working:
CPU generates page number → Check TLB:
• TLB Hit: Frame found immediately → 1 memory access total
• TLB Miss: Access page table in memory → 2 memory accesses total → update TLB

EAT formula: EAT = α(t+m) + (1-α)(t+2m)
where α = hit ratio, t = TLB access time, m = memory access time

Example: α=0.98, t=10ns, m=100ns
EAT = 0.98(110) + 0.02(210) = 107.8 + 4.2 = 112 ns (vs 200ns without TLB)
Q3Compare Best Fit, First Fit and Worst Fit allocation strategies. (6 marks)
See comparison table above. Key exam points:

• First Fit — Fastest (stops at first suitable hole). Best for speed.
• Best Fit — Least waste per allocation but creates many tiny holes and requires full list search.
• Worst Fit — Produces largest leftover hole (may accommodate future requests) but worst overall performance.

Conclusion from simulations: First Fit and Best Fit both outperform Worst Fit. First Fit is generally preferred due to speed.
Q4What is Thrashing? How is it caused and prevented? (5 marks)
Thrashing: A process spends more time in paging than actual execution. CPU utilization drops sharply.

Cause: Increasing degree of multiprogramming beyond a point reduces frames per process below the working set → frequent page faults → OS spends all time swapping pages.

Prevention:
1. Working Set Model: Track pages used in last Δ references (working set). Allocate enough frames to hold each process's working set. If Σ(working set sizes) > total frames → suspend a process.
2. Page Fault Frequency (PFF) scheme: If page fault rate is too high → give more frames. If too low → take away frames.
Q5Apply FIFO and LRU to: Ref string = 1,2,3,4,1,2,5,1,2,3,4,5; Frames = 3 (8 marks)
FIFO (3 frames):
1→[1] F | 2→[1,2] F | 3→[1,2,3] F | 4→[4,2,3] F(rep1) | 1→[4,1,3] F(rep2) | 2→[4,1,2] F(rep3) | 5→[5,1,2] F(rep4) | 1→H | 2→H | 3→[5,3,2] F(rep1) | 4→[5,3,4] F(rep2) | 5→H
FIFO page faults = 9

LRU (3 frames):
1→[1] F | 2→[1,2] F | 3→[1,2,3] F | 4→[2,3,4] F(rep1) | 1→[3,4,1] F(rep2) | 2→[4,1,2] F(rep3) | 5→[1,2,5] F(rep4) | 1→H | 2→H | 3→[2,5,3] F(rep1) | 4→[5,3,4] F(rep2) | 5→H
LRU page faults = 8

LRU performs better here. OPT would give even fewer faults.
CHAPTER 06 · CO6
File Management & I/O Management
File Organization · Directory Structure · I/O Devices · DMA · Disk Scheduling
File System Concepts
What is a File?
A file is a named collection of related information stored on secondary storage. It's the smallest logical unit of storage from the user's perspective.

File Attributes: Name, Type, Location (pointer to device/location), Size, Protection (permissions), Timestamps (created/modified/accessed), Owner

File Operations: Create, Read, Write, Reposition (seek), Delete, Truncate, Open, Close
File Organization & Access Methods
1. Sequential Access — Read bytes/records in order (like magnetic tape). Position maintained with a pointer. Most common.
Operations: read_next(), write_next(), reset()

2. Direct (Random) Access — Any block can be accessed directly using block number. Like a disk.
Operations: read(n), write(n), position_to(n)

3. Indexed Sequential Access — Index structure built on sequential file. Use index to find region, then sequential search within.
Directory Structure
StructureDescriptionProsCons
Single LevelAll files in one directory. All users share one namespace.Simple to implementNaming conflicts between users; hard to organize
Two LevelSeparate directory for each user (MFD = Master, UFD = User)Isolation between usersNo sharing between users; no subdirectories
Tree StructureHierarchical — root → directories → subdirectories → filesEfficient search; organized; used in Unix/WindowsSharing across branches is harder
Acyclic GraphAllows shared files/dirs via links (symbolic/hard links)Sharing without duplicationDangling pointers if original deleted; complex
Path Names
Absolute path: From root → /home/user/docs/file.txt
Relative path: From current directory → ../docs/file.txt

Special entries: "." = current directory, ".." = parent directory
I/O Management
I/O Devices & Categories
Block devices: Store data in fixed-size blocks, each addressable (HDD, SSD, DVD). Support read/write/seek.
Character devices: Deliver/accept stream of bytes (keyboard, mouse, serial port). No addressing.
Network devices: Socket interface (TCP/IP)

Hard Disk Structure: Platter → Track → Sector (512 bytes to 4KB). Access time = Seek time + Rotational latency + Transfer time
Modes of I/O Transfer
1. Programmed I/O (Polling): CPU continuously checks (polls) device status register. Wastes CPU cycles. Used for slow devices or simple systems.

2. Interrupt-Driven I/O: CPU initiates I/O, then continues other work. Device interrupts CPU when done. More efficient than polling. Used in most modern systems.

3. DMA (Direct Memory Access): Special DMA controller handles bulk data transfer between device and memory without CPU involvement. CPU only initiates and is interrupted at completion. Most efficient for large data transfers (disk, network).
📊 DMA vs Programmed I/O vs Interrupt
Programmed I/O: CPU → issues I/O → keeps checking (polling) → gets data CPU utilization: LOW (busy waiting) Interrupt-Driven I/O: CPU → issues I/O → does other work → INTERRUPT → handles data CPU utilization: MEDIUM-HIGH DMA: CPU → tells DMA controller (address, count, direction) DMA → transfers data directly to/from memory (no CPU) CPU → INTERRUPT only when transfer complete CPU utilization: HIGHEST ✓ Best for large transfers
Disk Scheduling Algorithms
Goal of Disk Scheduling
Minimize seek time (arm movement) to serve disk read/write requests efficiently. Given a queue of track requests, find the best order to service them.

Disk access time = Seek time + Rotational latency + Transfer time
Seek time dominates → that's what we optimize.
AlgorithmStrategyHead MovementIssue
FCFSService in arrival orderHigh (back & forth)Not optimal; fair
SSTFService closest request to current headLow usuallyStarvation (far requests may never be served)
SCAN (Elevator)Head moves in one direction, services all requests, then reversesMediumRequests at far end wait longer
C-SCANLike SCAN but returns to beginning without servicing on return sweepMediumMore uniform wait times than SCAN
LOOKLike SCAN but only goes to last request, doesn't go to disk endLower than SCANBetter than SCAN
C-LOOKLike C-SCAN but only goes to last request in each directionLowestBest of LOOK variants
📊 Disk Scheduling Example — Requests: 82,170,43,140,24,16,190 | Head at: 50 | Moving towards higher
FCFS: 50→82→170→43→140→24→16→190 Total head movement = 32+88+127+97+116+8+174 = 642 cylinders SSTF: 50→43→24→16→82→140→170→190 ↑closest each time Total = 7+19+8+66+58+30+20 = 208 cylinders SCAN: 50→82→140→170→190→(reverse)→43→24→16 Total = 32+58+30+20+147+19+8 = 314 cylinders C-SCAN: 50→82→140→170→190→(jump to 0)→16→24→43 Total = (50→190) + jump + (0→43) = 140 + 190 + 43 = 373 cylinders (but more uniform wait times) LOOK: 50→82→140→170→190→43→24→16 Total = 140 + 174 = 314 cylinders Best: SSTF (208) but risks starvation; LOOK/C-LOOK best balance
💡
Which to recommend? SSTF has best seek time but starvation risk. SCAN/LOOK better for heavily loaded systems. C-SCAN/C-LOOK when uniform wait times are needed. FCFS only when requests are rarely queued.
Important Q&A
Q1Explain the different types of directory structures with diagrams described. (8 marks)
1. Single Level Directory: All files in one directory for all users. Simple but causes naming conflicts and disorganized as files grow.

2. Two Level Directory: OS maintains a Master File Directory (MFD). Each user gets their own User File Directory (UFD). Solves naming conflicts but no subdirectories or easy sharing.

3. Tree-Structured Directory: Hierarchical structure with a root, branching into subdirectories and files. Used in UNIX, Windows. Supports absolute and relative paths. Efficient but sharing files across branches requires copying or links.

4. Acyclic Graph Directory: Allows directories and files to be shared via links (hard link = points to inode; symbolic/soft link = path reference). Enables sharing without duplication. Problem: dangling pointers when original is deleted. Solution: reference counting (delete only when count = 0).
Q2What is DMA? How does it work? Advantages? (6 marks)
DMA (Direct Memory Access) is a hardware mechanism that allows I/O devices to transfer data directly to/from main memory without involving the CPU for each byte.

Working:
1. CPU writes DMA command block (source address, destination address, byte count, direction)
2. CPU resumes other work
3. DMA controller handles the data transfer byte by byte (or block by block) via memory bus
4. When done, DMA controller interrupts CPU
5. CPU handles the interrupt (cleanup/processing)

Advantages:
• Frees CPU during bulk data transfer
• Faster for large transfers (disk, network cards)
• CPU only involved at start and end

Disadvantage: DMA and CPU compete for memory bus → "cycle stealing"
Q3Apply SCAN and C-SCAN to: Queue = 98,183,37,122,14,124,65,67. Head = 53. Moving towards higher. (8 marks)
Sort requests: 14, 37, 65, 67, 98, 122, 124, 183
Head at 53, moving towards higher.

SCAN:
Higher direction first: 53→65→67→98→122→124→183
Then reverse: 183→37→14
Movement: (65-53)+(67-65)+(98-67)+(122-98)+(124-122)+(183-124) + (183-14)
= 12+2+31+24+2+59 + (183→37: 146 + 37-14: 23) = 130 + 169 = 299 cylinders

C-SCAN:
Higher direction: 53→65→67→98→122→124→183
Jump to 0, then service: 0→14→37
Movement = (183-53) + (183-0) + (37-0) = 130 + 183 + 37 = 350 cylinders
(But wait time is more uniform — no one in lower range waits as long)
Q4Differentiate FCFS, SSTF and LOOK disk scheduling. (6 marks)
FCFS (First Come First Serve): Services requests in order of arrival. Simple and fair. High total head movement as head jumps back and forth across the disk. No starvation.

SSTF (Shortest Seek Time First): Always services the request nearest the current head position. Minimizes individual seek times and total movement. However, requests far from the head can starve if new closer requests keep arriving.

LOOK: Head moves in one direction serving all requests until no more in that direction, then reverses (doesn't go all the way to end like SCAN). More efficient than SCAN. No starvation. Good all-around performer for loaded disks.
Q5Explain the three modes of I/O data transfer. (6 marks)
1. Programmed I/O (Polling): CPU directly controls I/O. After issuing command, CPU continuously checks (polls) a status register waiting for I/O to complete. Very wasteful — CPU is stuck in a busy-wait loop. Used only in embedded/simple systems.

2. Interrupt-Driven I/O: CPU issues I/O command and then continues executing other processes. When the device completes the operation, it sends a hardware interrupt to the CPU. CPU saves state, runs interrupt handler, resumes. More efficient as CPU can do useful work while I/O proceeds. Used for character devices (keyboard, mouse).

3. DMA (Direct Memory Access): CPU programs a DMA controller with source, destination, and size. DMA transfers data between device and memory directly via the memory bus, without per-byte CPU involvement. Interrupts CPU only when entire transfer is done. Best for bulk transfers. Used for disks, sound cards, network adapters.
Q6What is file organization? Compare sequential, direct and indexed sequential access. (5 marks)
File Organization refers to how data is stored and accessed within a file.

Sequential Access: Records read in order, one after another. Pointer (current_position) advances with each read/write. Must rewind to re-read earlier records. Simple, works for all media (tapes, disks).

Direct (Random) Access: Any record can be read/written directly by specifying its block number. No need to read preceding records. Works only on direct-access devices (disks, not tapes). Ideal for databases.

Indexed Sequential Access: Builds an index over a sequential file. Index stores key + pointer. To find a record: binary search the index → get pointer → sequential search from that point. Good balance between random and sequential access. Used in ISAM (Indexed Sequential Access Method).
